clean_story: Empty time.  A mind seeking friction. "Boredom" comes from the Old French borer—meaning "to vex or annoy"—because stillness, when unwanted, feels like an itch without a scratch. If boredom can rightly be perceived as a signal, it’s the brain’s idle alert, like a car engine sputtering in neutral. Not a lack of stimuli, but a mismatch:too little challenge for your skills, or too much repetition without reward. Studies show prison inmates and factory workers report higher boredom than farmers—though all face monotony. The difference?Farmers track seasons, weather, growth. Their "repetition" has invisible variables. Boredom thrives on predictability. In 2014, researchers stranded volunteers in a room with only their thoughts for 15 minutes. 67% chose electric shocks over silence. Not because pain was pleasant—but unpredictability broke the stagnation. Children complain of boredom most. Their brains crave novelty to build neural pathways. Adults call it "restlessness" and drown it in scrolling—same impulse, different tool. Three related subjects are attention spans, flow states, and sensory deprivation.
